title: Wired Wrong
editorialSummary: Sixteen well-known cognitive biases grouped by the class of mental error they represent. Anchoring and framing errors (the mind locks onto an initial reference point or presentation): Anchoring Bias, Framing Effect, Contrast Effect, Decoy Effect. Social and conformity errors (judgements distorted by other people): Bandwagon Effect, Halo Effect, Authority Bias, In-Group Bias. Memory distortion errors (recall is reconstructed rather than played back accurately): Hindsight Bias, Rosy Retrospection, Source Confusion, Misinformation Effect. Probability and risk misjudgements (faulty intuitions about likelihood and chance): Gambler's Fallacy, Base Rate Neglect, Availability Heuristic, Hot-Hand Fallacy. The mild false trail is Halo Effect: its name evokes something warm and memory-like (a glowing after-image), so players may place it in the memory group; but it is a social-perception bias — judging a person's abilities from one positive trait — firmly in the social/conformity cluster. All 16 items are standard vocabulary in popular psychology and widely covered in mainstream media, so no item requires specialist knowledge.
